message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 7
Recent Green phase: Added component-block logic. quote() now splits items into mainItems and components (rune), prices components via componentBasePremium(count) which charges COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM (60) per full block of 3 and COMPONENT_BASE_PREMIUM (25) for the remainder, multiplies both main and component base by FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER, then adds PROCESSING_FEE and rounds up.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. The quote function filters items twice (once for main, once to count components) — consider whether that duplication is worth removing. Do not add behavior for future tests (no moonstone yet — only rune is a component so far; no "alike per type" grouping beyond what's implemented; no modifiers, no claim logic). Keep COMPONENT_TYPES as the single source of truth for which types are components.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 7 tests pass. Now let me analyze the code for refactoring.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing the names:
- `quote`, `componentBasePremium`, `roundUpInMHPCOFavor` — all clearly reveal intent.
- `mainItems`, `componentCount`, `mainBase`, `componentBase` — descriptive.
- The constants `COMPONENT_TYPES`, `COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE`, etc. are clear.

Naming is already strong. No rename needed.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (the `quote` function)

The prompt specifically flags the **double filter** duplication: `quote` filters `items` once for `mainItems` and again to count components. This is the prime refactoring target (Rule 3: No Duplication — there's a conceptual single-pass partition being done as two passes).

Let me compute mass of the current `quote` body:

```
const mainItems = items.filter((item) => !COMPONENT_TYPES.includes(item.type));
const componentCount = items.filter((item) => COMPONENT_TYPES.includes(item.type)).length;
const mainBase = mainItems.reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type] * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER, 0);
const componentBase = componentBasePremium(componentCount) * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER;
return roundUpInMHPCOFavor(mainBase + componentBase + PROCESSING_FEE);
```

Counting components:
- Bindings (mainItems, componentCount, mainBase, componentBase, item×3, sum, count refs): ~ scalars/params. Let me count the heavy contributors.
- Invocations: `filter`, `includes`, `filter`, `includes`, `.length` (binding), `reduce`, `BASE_PREMIUMS[]` access, `*`, `componentBasePremium()`, `*`, `roundUpInMHPCOFavor()`, `+`, `+` → roughly 12 invocations × 2 = 24
- Loops: 2 filters + 1 reduce = 3 loops × 5 = 15
- Assignments (bindings via const are bindings not mutations; the `sum` accumulator is functional). Treating `const` as bindings (mass 1).

The dominant cost is **3 iterations over `items`** (15 mass from loops) plus duplicated `COMPONENT_TYPES.includes` invocations. Removing the duplicate filter cuts one loop (−5 mass) and one `includes` invocation pair.

## Step 3: Apply Rule 3 — Remove Duplication

I'll partition `items` in a single pass instead of filtering twice. A single `reduce`/loop that splits into main and component groups removes the duplicated filter predicate and one iteration. I'll keep `COMPONENT_TYPES` as the single source of truth via a small `isComponent` helper, which also names the predicate intent (Rule 2 bonus).

content.text: This removes the duplicated `COMPONENT_TYPES.includes` filter predicate and eliminates the second iteration over `items`. Since every item is either a main item or a component, `componentCount` is simply the items that weren't kept as main items — derived arithmetically rather than via a second loop. Let me verify tests stay green.

content.text:
All 7 tests still pass.

## Step 5: APP Mass Comparison

**Before** (component-counting portion):
```
const mainItems = items.filter((item) => !COMPONENT_TYPES.includes(item.type));
const componentCount = items.filter((item) => COMPONENT_TYPES.includes(item.type)).length;
```
- Loops: 2 filters = 2 × 5 = 10
- Invocations: `filter`, `includes`, `!`, `filter`, `includes`, `.length` access ≈ 5 × 2 = 10
- Bindings: mainItems, componentCount, item×2 = 4

**After**:
```
const mainItems = items.filter((item) => !isComponent(item));
const componentCount = items.length - mainItems.length;
```
- Loops: 1 filter = 1 × 5 = 5
- Invocations: `filter`, `isComponent`, `!`, `-` ≈ 4 × 2 = 8
- Bindings: mainItems, componentCount, item = 3

The extracted `isComponent` helper adds a small constant (one binding + `includes` invocation) but is invoked from a single place and replaces the inline predicate, so it nets out roughly even while removing the duplicated predicate.

**Net mass change for the partition logic: −5 (eliminated one full loop over `items`) plus removal of the duplicated `COMPONENT_TYPES.includes` predicate.**

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied** (Rule 3 — No Duplication, the change the prompt flagged):
- Removed the second `items.filter(... COMPONENT_TYPES.includes ...)` pass. `componentCount` is now derived as `items.length - mainItems.length`, since every item is either a main item or a component. This eliminates one iteration over `items` (−5 mass) and the duplicated predicate.
- Extracted `isComponent(item)` as a named predicate. This keeps `COMPONENT_TYPES` as the single source of truth (used in exactly one place now) and gives the partition criterion an intent-revealing name (Rule 2 bonus).

Rules evaluated:
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): all 7 green before and after.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): names already strong; `isComponent` adds clarity. No renames needed.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): the targeted duplicate filter is removed.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): one fewer loop; the new helper is justified as it is referenced and removes inline duplication.

No future behavior was added (no moonstone, no per-type grouping, no modifiers, no claim logic).

---

Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Removed the duplicated component filter in `quote` — `componentCount` now derived arithmetically from `items.length - mainItems.length`; extracted intent-revealing `isComponent` predicate keeping `COMPONENT_TYPES` as the single source of truth.
- **Mass Change**: partition logic reduced by ~5 (one fewer iteration over `items`) plus removal of the duplicated `includes` predicate.
- **Tests**: All 7 passing.

File: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_18-30-07_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
